preamble: The shift from the voiceless labiodental fricative [f] to the voiced bilabial stop [b] involves both fortition (strengthening) and voicing. It is relatively rare as a regular historical sound law but is very common in loanword adaptation when a language lacks native labiodental fricatives, and in child language acquisition.

examples.note: Languages lacking native /f/ or /v/ often adapt them using the closest native stop, typically [b] or [p].
Children frequently substitute easier-to-articulate stops for fricatives in a process known as 'stopping'.
